title: coinbase-payout: index.ts bridge — onShare to leaderboard wiring; round lifecycle; split gating behind feature flag + operator legal ack; persist mining_block_payouts snapshot on block accept (M)
description: Per docs/COINBASE-PAYOUT-POOL-DESIGN.md. Wire onShare events into leaderboard.ts; manage mining_rounds lifecycle (open a round, close it when a block is found, open the next); gate the entire split-payout code path behind a feature flag AND an operator legal-acknowledgment record (see disclosures bead) so it cannot be silently enabled; on accepted block, persist the mining_block_payouts snapshot (what actually paid, per output) for audit. Size: M.

title: coinbase-payout: Admin mining UI — mode toggle, percentage inputs + validation, fee address, N, live split preview, legal-acknowledgment gate (M)
description: Per docs/COINBASE-PAYOUT-POOL-DESIGN.md and Cairn UX philosophy (plain language, no Bitcoin internals exposed, GUI-testable). Admin surface for split-mode config: mode toggle solo/split, finder/fee percentage inputs with inline validation, fee address field, leaderboard N (slot count), a live preview showing exactly how a hypothetical block reward would split across outputs, and a gate that requires reading + accepting the legal acknowledgment (see disclosures bead) before the toggle can be flipped on. Size: M.

title: coinbase-payout: User mining view — split-mode earnings display (finder %, your rank, estimated slot value); round history from mining_block_payouts (M)
description: Per docs/COINBASE-PAYOUT-POOL-DESIGN.md and Cairn UX philosophy (plain language, no Bitcoin internals exposed). Miner-facing view for split mode: current finder percentage, the user's live rank on the round leaderboard, an estimated value of their current slot if the round ended now, and a round-history list sourced from mining_block_payouts showing what was actually paid each round. Size: M.

title: coinbase-payout: Disclosures — split-mode operator acknowledgment record mirroring admin_disclosure_acceptances (S)
description: Per docs/COINBASE-PAYOUT-POOL-DESIGN.md. Add a split-mode operator legal-acknowledgment record, mirroring the existing admin_disclosure_acceptances pattern, that the Admin mining UI toggle gate (cairn-l1zu.13) checks before allowing split mode to be enabled. Size: S.

title: coinbase-payout: Federation-ready seams — stable local instanceId from federation identity seed (no networking, F15 respected); (instanceId,userId) keying; versioned-JSON PayoutSet wire serializer (S)
description: Per docs/COINBASE-PAYOUT-POOL-DESIGN.md. Phase-1, single-instance work that keeps phase-2 federation cheap: derive a stable local instanceId from the federation identity seed (no networking involved yet, respects federation security gate F15 from epic cairn-cz3q); key internal leaderboard/round state by (instanceId, userId) even though instanceId is constant for now; write a versioned-JSON wire serializer for PayoutSet so it can later be gossiped between instances without a breaking format change. Size: S.

title: coinbase-payout: QA GATE — forced-solve regtest e2e, multi-output block accepted by bitcoind, every output pays exact expected sats, conservation holds (M)
description: Per docs/COINBASE-PAYOUT-POOL-DESIGN.md. Regtest e2e test using the existing forced-solve harness (cairn-vn43.2 lineage): force a split-mode block solve, submit it to bitcoind, assert the block is accepted, assert every coinbase output pays exactly the expected sats per the active PayoutSet (finder pct, fee pct, each leaderboard slot), and assert sats conservation (sum of outputs plus fees equals block subsidy plus tx fees, no dust leakage, no rounding drift). This is a merge-blocker for the whole epic -- nothing in split mode ships without this passing. Size: M.

title: coinbase-payout follow-up: leaderboard payout address rotation (privacy — recipients currently reuse one address across coinbases, same as Ocean) (S)
description: Per docs/COINBASE-PAYOUT-POOL-DESIGN.md. Known privacy limitation carried over from phase-1 design (matches Ocean's behavior): leaderboard slot recipients reuse the same payout address across every coinbase they appear in, which is address-reuse and links their mining activity on-chain. Not a blocker for phase-1 ship; scope out per-round or per-block address rotation for leaderboard payouts as a follow-up. Size: S, low priority.

title: Decide finder % default: economics recommends 40% (range 20–60 configurable); Alex proposed 50% (in range, stay-biased); optional size-tiering 50/40/30
description: Per docs/COINBASE-PAYOUT-POOL-DESIGN.md economics section. The finder percentage is the share of the block reward that goes to whoever actually found the block (vs. the top-N leaderboard pool). Economic modeling recommends a 40% default with a configurable 20-60% range to keep finder incentive and pool stickiness balanced. Alex has proposed 50% as the default instead -- it is inside the recommended range and biases toward rewarding solo-style luck (stay-biased), which may aid retention. There is also an optional size-tiering idea (50/40/30 finder pct depending on operator/pool size tier) worth a yes/no. Needs Alex's decision before settings.ts (cairn-l1zu.11) default values and Admin UI (cairn-l1zu.13) copy are finalized.

title: coinbase-payout: types.ts — PayoutSet, LeaderboardEntry, required ShareEvent.realizedHashValue, SolveEvent payout-set ref (M)
description: Design types per docs/COINBASE-PAYOUT-POOL-DESIGN.md. Add PayoutSet (ordered list of {scriptHex/address, sats or pct, role}) and LeaderboardEntry types; make ShareEvent.realizedHashValue required (currently optional/absent); add a payout-set reference to SolveEvent so a solved block records exactly which PayoutSet paid it. Size: M.
close_reason: Accidental duplicate created by a broken script pipe during bead creation in this same session; canonical types.ts bead is cairn-l1zu.3 (identical title/description, correctly wired to legal gate + downstream deps). No other bead depends on cairn-l1zu.2.

title: Decide top-N pot distribution: equal-split vs proportional-to-since-round-start-work (economics strongly recommends the latter; compatible with no-decay ruling)
description: Per docs/COINBASE-PAYOUT-POOL-DESIGN.md economics section. Once the top-N leaderboard slots are determined, the non-finder pot can be split either equally across the N slots, or proportionally to each slot-holder's realized work since the round started. Economic modeling strongly recommends proportional-to-work: it better rewards actual contribution and reduces incentive to game slot N with a single lucky share. This is compatible with the earlier no-decay/beat-only ruling for leaderboard.ts (cairn-l1zu.8) -- proportional weighting can use the same monotonic best-share values already being tracked. Needs Alex's decision to fix the ScoreFn/default distribution before leaderboard.ts and settings.ts (leaderboard_rank) are finalized.

title: Decide minimum-share-count qualification m_min (the effective sybil lever — makes fake identities cost real hashrate)
description: Per docs/COINBASE-PAYOUT-POOL-DESIGN.md. To qualify for a top-N leaderboard slot, a user should be required to submit at least m_min shares in the round. This is the primary sybil-resistance lever for the leaderboard: without a minimum, an attacker could register many identities and hope one gets lucky on a single share, for near-zero real hashrate cost; with a sensible m_min, gaming the leaderboard requires actually contributing real hashrate. Needs Alex's decision on m_min (or the formula/heuristic for deriving it, e.g. relative to expected shares-per-round at current difficulty) before leaderboard.ts (cairn-l1zu.8) qualification logic and settings.ts defaults are finalized.

title: Federation mining: meta-leaderboard across Heartwood instances (phase 2, design-only)
description: Phase 2 of the coinbase-payout pool design (docs/COINBASE-PAYOUT-POOL-DESIGN.md) -- design-only for now, not phase-1 implementation scope. ShareAnnounce gossip between Heartwood instances with independent PoW verification per announced share; payout address is trustless via out[0]-in-PoW (the address is committed inside the proof-of-work itself, so a receiving instance can verify a remote share's claimed payout target without trusting the announcing instance); per-instance slot caps so one instance can't dominate the meta-leaderboard; meta-rank by summed work across instances (note: best-share-only ranking does NOT survive untrusted instances the way it does single-instance -- a hostile instance could lie about its best share, so meta-ranking needs summed/verified work instead); finder-instance-takes-fee (the instance that actually found the block keeps the operator fee, not the meta-pool); per-instance round bookkeeping. Open questions on gossip protocol, verification cost, and abuse cases are tracked in the design doc's federation section -- this bead is for scoping/design, not code.

comments.author: alexl
comments.text: Design-only scoping COMPLETE in docs/COINBASE-POOL-ANALYSIS-ADDENDUM-2026-07-19.md section 4 (concrete meta-leaderboard design sketch). Answers every item the bead asks: gossip protocol (4.1 ShareAnnounce + boardMerkleRoot commitment), verification cost (4.2, ~O(merkle depth) per share, ~10/sec across 10 instances, bounded by static meta_min_diff), slot caps (4.4 cap M), meta-rank by summed VERIFIED work (4.4, best-share does NOT survive untrusted instances per C-3), finder-instance-takes-fee (4.4), and abuse cases (4.5: meta-sybil, template dishonesty, offline slot-squat, gossip partition, share-flood DoS - each bounded, never funds). CORRECTS the bead's own 'per-instance round bookkeeping' assumption: analysis C-4 proved per-instance rounds + a federation meta-board incoherent; superseded here by the global on-chain round (reset on any federation instance's block confirming on the Bitcoin chain). Also adds the work-prop-symmetry invariant (section 3.3): the meta split rule MUST equal the intra-instance split rule, because work-prop-single-instance + equal-split-meta reconstructs the single worst combination tested (C-3, 94% capture at x=0.5). Design scope done; implementation stays gated on legal gate l1zu.1 + F15 networking gate. CLOSING.

title: Re-implement coinbase-pool Monte-Carlo sims as checked-in reproducible code (docs/COINBASE-POOL-ANALYSIS.md §3 provenance gap)
description: The Monte-Carlo simulation results reported throughout docs/COINBASE-POOL-ANALYSIS.md (esp. §3 single-instance economics, §5 edge cases, §6 federation) were produced by scratchpad code (sims/ and fed-sims/, numpy/scipy, Python 3.13) that was NEVER checked into the repo and no longer exists. The numeric findings are therefore point-in-time results from unretained code and cannot currently be re-run or independently verified. Task: re-implement the simulations as checked-in, seeded, reproducible code (a scripts/ or docs/sims/ module) from the parameters described in the doc (§3 real inputs table: ~900 EH/s network, difficulty 127.17 T, 3.125 BTC subsidy, ~$62k BTC; share model B=W/E with E~Exp(1); pot-split rules equal vs work-proportional; finder-% sweep; hopper-survival; sybil/splitting; federation timescale/fee/sybil). Goal is to let anyone re-derive the tables in §3/§5/§6 and confirm they still hold. Do NOT change the doc's findings — this is a provenance/reproducibility gap, not a correctness dispute. Blocked-adjacent to the same legal gate (cairn-vn43.14 / epic cairn-l1zu) as all other coinbase-pool work, but this is doc-infrastructure and can proceed independently of the build decision.

comments.author: alexl
comments.text: Provenance down-payment in docs/COINBASE-POOL-ANALYSIS-ADDENDUM-2026-07-19.md section 5. The addendum's OWN new sims are documented reproducibly: Thread 1 m_min sensitivity (mmin_sensitivity.py, seed 20260719, 4M Exp(1) draws/cell) and Thread 2 split rounding (split_rounding.py, seed 424242, 200k random pots), each with recorded seeds, parameter tables, exact closed-form formulas, and MC-vs-closed-form cross-checks (inclusion matches exp(-c) within 3.8e-4; 0 conservation failures). numpy 2.5.1 / scipy 1.18.0 / py 3.13.7 matches the original stack. This is a TEMPLATE + partial coverage, NOT the full re-implementation. STAYS OPEN: the original section 3/5/6 economics + federation sims are still not re-created. Recommendation (orchestrator decides): preserve sim code under scripts/analysis/ or a docs appendix, never under src/ (analysis infra, unaffected by legal gate l1zu.1).

title: Send flow: no address-network validation — mainnet bc1 address accepted on regtest instance
description:
Wave-4A edge-case QA (chain-UP), qa2 worktree (v0.2.38), wallet /wallets/3/send.

Repro:
1. Single-sig P2WPKH wallet on a regtest-backed Heartwood instance (node at RPC 18543, regtest).
2. On the Send form, paste a MAINNET bech32 address: bc1qw508d6qejxtdg4y5r3zarvary0c5xw7kv8f3t4 (BIP-173 test vector).
3. UI immediately shows "Valid Bitcoin address" (green check) — no HRP/network check against the node's network.
4. Proceeds through Review (amount 0.001 BTC), last-4-char address confirmation, and all the way to
   "draft saved on your node" / signing step with a real PSBT built server-side sending to the mainnet address.
5. No inline error, no 5xx, no console error at any step — the gap is silent, not a crash.

Expected: address validation should check the HRP (bc1/tb1/bcrt1) against the instance's configured
network and reject/flag a network mismatch with a clear inline error, same as it presumably does for
malformed addresses.

Impact: on a REAL mainnet instance this same code path would presumably also accept a testnet/regtest/
signet address pasted by a confused user, which is an unrecoverable fund-loss vector (funds sent to an
address with no matching network are burned). Class P1 — validate on regtest before promoting risk
assessment, but the mainnet mirror-image is the actual danger.

Evidence: qa2-server.log shows no 4xx/5xx during the whole flow (checked via grep for status: (4|5)|ERROR
in the relevant window) — confirms this is a missing-validation bug, not a crash.

Not filed as a duplicate of cairn-8pl9w / cairn-v5ass (those are unit-drift beads, unrelated).
close_reason:
FALSE POSITIVE — retracting. This QA instance is deliberately configured with
network: 'mainnet' (hardcoded default, src/lib/server/settings.ts:269) even
though it's wired to a regtest bitcoind backend — this is the established
"bc1<->bcrt1 HRP trick" QA convention already documented in project memory
(cairn-msig-wave-2026-07-13: "team-mode gate, bc1->bcrt1 trick").

Re-tested with a genuine bcrt1 address on the same send form: it IS correctly
rejected with "That looks like a test-network address — this wallet uses
regular Bitcoin (mainnet)." This confirms address-network validation is
working correctly and consistently against the instance's configured network
(mainnet) — my original repro (bc1 address accepted) was actually the
CORRECT/expected behavior for this network config, not a validation gap.

No code or UX change needed. Apologies for the noise.

title: Per-user SMTP for email notifications
description:
Allow each user to configure their own SMTP credentials for email notifications, with the admin's instance-wide SMTP as fallback. Full design in docs/PER-USER-SMTP-PLAN.md.

Priority per send (resolved per-recipient, inside the email channel plugin): user's own SMTP > admin instance SMTP > email unavailable for that user (other channels unaffected).

Child beads, in dependency order:
1. Instance secret key + encryption helpers (secretKey.ts)
2. Email channel refactor: per-user SMTP resolution + fallback
3. Channel config API: email SMTP fields (save/redact/encrypt)
4. Test-before-save endpoint (test-smtp)
5. User settings UI: personal SMTP form
6. Admin SMTP UI: fallback explainer copy
7. Rollout verification (fresh install / existing install / Docker volume)

See docs/PER-USER-SMTP-PLAN.md for full architecture, including why no DB schema migration or notificationQueue.ts changes are needed (the queue already resolves send() per-recipient; resolution logic belongs in the email plugin, not the generic queue).
close_reason: All 7 child beads implemented, tested, and closed in dependency order (secretKey.ts encryption helpers → email channel per-user resolution → channel config API → test-smtp endpoint → user settings SMTP form → admin fallback copy → rollout verification). Also fixed cairn-vbnq (admin SMTP port .trim crash). No DB migration needed (additive JSON). 927 tests pass, typecheck clean, live end-to-end rollout verified.

title: Instance secret key + encryption helpers (secretKey.ts)
description:
Foundation for encrypting per-user SMTP passwords at rest. Everything else in this epic depends on this. See docs/PER-USER-SMTP-PLAN.md §2.

New module: src/lib/server/secretKey.ts

- getInstanceKey(): Buffer — on first call, if the key file doesn't exist, generate 32 random bytes (crypto.randomBytes(32)) and write them to a file colocated with the DB: path.join(path.dirname(DB_PATH), 'instance.key') (DB_PATH is exported from src/lib/server/db.ts — use its directory, NOT process.cwd(), so this works under Docker/Umbrel where the DB lives on a mounted volume). Write with mode 0o600. Cache the key in memory after first read.
  - IMPORTANT: this file must live under the same persistent volume as data/cairn.db. Verify this explicitly against the Docker/Umbrel deployment path, not just local dev — an ephemeral-storage mistake here silently orphans every saved SMTP password on container restart. This is the one easy way to get this bead wrong.
- encryptSecret(plaintext: string): string — AES-256-GCM. Derive the actual cipher key via HKDF-SHA256(getInstanceKey(), salt=none, info='cairn:notification-smtp-pass') so this use is domain-separated from any future use of the instance key for something else. Envelope format: small JSON object with base64 fields, versioned, e.g. { v: 1, iv, tag, data } — mirrors the envelope style in src/lib/server/backup.ts (encryptBackup/decryptBackup) but without a passphrase/KDF step, since the instance key is already high-entropy.
- decryptSecret(envelope: string): string — inverse of the above. Throw a clear error on a malformed/wrong-version envelope (don't silently return garbage).

Acceptance criteria:
- Unit tests: round-trip encrypt/decrypt; tampering with any envelope field (iv/tag/data) fails to decrypt (GCM auth tag catches it); key file is generated exactly once and reused on subsequent calls within a process; file permissions are restrictive (skip the permission-bits assertion on Windows CI if needed, but keep it for POSIX).
- Confirm getInstanceKey() derives its file path from DB_PATH's directory, with a test or explicit code comment proving this (not process.cwd()).
- No changes to any other file in this bead — this is a standalone, side-effect-free (until first call) module.
close_reason: Implemented src/lib/server/secretKey.ts (getInstanceKey lazy-gen colocated with DB_PATH, AES-256-GCM via HKDF-SHA256, versioned envelope). Exported DB_PATH from db.ts. 10 unit tests pass (round-trip, tamper detection on iv/tag/data, single-generation+reuse, POSIX 0600 perms, DB-dir path derivation).

title: Email channel: per-user SMTP resolution + fallback
description:
Depends on: secretKey.ts bead (instance secret key + encryption helpers).

Refactor src/lib/server/channels/email.ts so each send resolves SMTP config per-recipient instead of always reading the instance-wide settings. See docs/PER-USER-SMTP-PLAN.md §3.

- Extend the EmailChannelConfig interface (currently just { address?: string }) with an optional smtp field:
    smtp?: { host: string; port: number; user: string | null; from: string; tls: 'starttls'|'tls'|'none'; passEnc: string | null }
  This is stored in the SAME notification_channel_config.config JSON blob (channel='email') that already holds `address` — no new table/column. Old rows without `smtp` must keep working exactly as today (smtp absent = no personal SMTP = fall back to instance).
- Change readSmtpConfig() (currently no-arg, reads only instance settings) to readSmtpConfig(userId: number):
  1. Read the user's notification_channel_config row for channel='email'. If it has a `smtp` sub-object, decrypt smtp.passEnc via decryptSecret() (from the secretKey.ts bead) and return that as the SmtpConfig.
  2. Otherwise fall back to today's instance-settings read (getSetting('smtp_host') etc.) — this logic already exists, just keep it as the fallback branch.
  3. If neither resolves, return null (unchanged behavior).
- Thread userId through: isConfigured(userId), send(userId, payload), test(userId), and the internal deliver(userId, payload) all already receive userId — just pass it into the new readSmtpConfig(userId) instead of the old no-arg call.
- Update the "SMTP not configured" error message in deliver() to reflect that the user could set up their own: "No SMTP configured — set up your own in Settings › Notifications, or ask your admin to configure instance email."
- Add a shared helper `sendTestWithConfig(userId: number, smtp: SmtpConfig): Promise<ChannelSendResult>` that builds a transporter directly from a given SmtpConfig and sends the canned test payload — refactor the existing test() to call it with the resolved config, so the test-before-save endpoint bead can reuse the exact same send/error-classification path with a candidate (not-yet-saved) config.
- Do NOT touch notificationQueue.ts — it already calls plugin.send(row.user_id, payload) per row; no changes needed there (confirmed in docs/PER-USER-SMTP-PLAN.md §1).

Acceptance criteria:
- Existing email.ts tests (email.test.ts, email.smtp-integration.test.ts) still pass, updated for the new userId-aware readSmtpConfig.
- New test: a user with a saved personal SMTP config gets mail routed through it even when instance SMTP is also configured (personal wins).
- New test: a user with no personal SMTP falls back to instance SMTP unchanged from today's behavior.
- New test: neither configured → ok:false, retryable:false, with the updated message.
- New test: decryption failure (corrupt passEnc) is handled as a clear non-retryable error, not a thrown exception that crashes the queue tick.
close_reason: Refactored email.ts: readSmtpConfig(userId) resolves personal SMTP (decrypting passEnc) → instance fallback → null. Added deliverWith (shared send/error path), sendTestWithConfig export, resolveSmtp (maps decrypt-failure + no-config to non-retryable results). isConfigured uses non-decrypting smtpIsAvailable. Updated NO_SMTP_MESSAGE. Exported SmtpConfig/PersonalSmtp types. 6 new tests + existing email.test/smtp-integration all pass (19 total).

title: Channel config API: email SMTP fields (save/redact/encrypt)
description:
Depends on: secretKey.ts bead, Email channel refactor bead (needs the EmailChannelConfig.smtp shape it defines).

Extend src/routes/api/notifications/channels/[channel]/+server.ts so users can save/view their personal SMTP config through the existing generic PUT/GET/DELETE endpoints. See docs/PER-USER-SMTP-PLAN.md §6.

- buildConfig('email', body, prev): currently only handles `address`. Add handling for an optional `smtp` object in the request body: { host, port, user, from, tls, pass }.
  - Validate host and from are non-empty, tls is one of 'starttls'|'tls'|'none', port is an integer 1-65535 — mirror the exact validation already in src/routes/api/admin/notifications/+server.ts for the equivalent instance-wide fields.
  - pass: blank/absent means "keep the stored password" — carry forward prev's smtp.passEnc unchanged (same blank-means-keep convention already used for ntfy.accessToken and webhook.secret in this same file). Non-blank pass gets encrypted via encryptSecret() (from the secretKey.ts bead) into passEnc before storing — the plaintext password must never be written to the config JSON.
  - If the request omits `smtp` entirely, leave any previously-saved smtp config untouched (don't silently delete personal SMTP just because a request only updated `address`).
  - Provide a way to explicitly remove personal SMTP without deleting the whole email channel config (e.g. an explicit `clearSmtp: true` flag in the body) — deleting the whole row via DELETE already removes both address override and SMTP together, which is coarser than a user just wanting to go back to instance-fallback while keeping their custom destination address.
- redactForClient('email', cfg): currently a no-op passthrough. Add: strip smtp.passEnc entirely from the object returned to the browser, replace with smtp.hasPass: boolean — same pattern as the existing ntfy/webhook redaction in this file.
- GET response for channel='email' already includes `defaults.address`; no change needed there.

Acceptance criteria:
- PUT with a new smtp object (including pass) persists it with pass encrypted (verify by reading the raw DB row in a test — passEnc must not equal the plaintext, and the plaintext must not appear anywhere in the stored JSON).
- PUT with smtp.pass blank after a prior save keeps the previously-encrypted passEnc.
- GET never returns smtp.passEnc, only smtp.hasPass.
- PUT with clearSmtp:true removes only the smtp sub-object, leaving `address` (if any) intact.
- Existing tests for telegram/ntfy/nostr/webhook channels on this same route are unaffected (this bead only touches the 'email' case).
close_reason: Extended channels/[channel]/+server.ts: buildConfig('email') now accepts optional smtp sub-object (validated via new buildEmailSmtp mirroring admin validation), encrypts pass via encryptSecret, blank-pass keeps prev passEnc, clearSmtp:true drops smtp keeping address, omitted smtp preserves prior. redactForClient('email') strips passEnc → hasPass. Address now optional when SMTP present. 10 route tests pass (encryption-at-rest, blank-keep, clearSmtp, redaction, validation, other-channels unaffected).

title: Test-before-save endpoint for personal SMTP
description:
Depends on: Email channel refactor bead (needs sendTestWithConfig helper), Channel config API bead (needs the smtp field shape/encryption convention it establishes, for the blank-pass-means-use-stored-password case).

New route: src/routes/api/notifications/channels/email/test-smtp/+server.ts (POST). See docs/PER-USER-SMTP-PLAN.md §4.

Why a new route instead of reusing the existing generic POST /api/notifications/channels/[channel]/test: that route tests the ALREADY-SAVED config (plugin.test(userId) reads from DB). We need to test candidate form values before the user has saved anything, so a typo'd password doesn't get persisted as "verified" and so the user gets feedback before committing.

- requireUser(event) same as the other notification routes.
- Body: { host, port, user, pass, from, tls }. Validate the same way buildConfig('email', ...) does in the channel config API bead (host/from required, tls enum, port range).
- pass blank/absent: fall back to the user's already-saved encrypted password (decrypt via decryptSecret() from the saved notification_channel_config row) so re-testing after a first successful save doesn't require re-entering the password. If there's no saved password either, treat as no-auth SMTP (some relays allow this) rather than erroring.
- Call the shared `sendTestWithConfig(userId, candidateSmtpConfig)` helper added in the email channel refactor bead — do NOT duplicate the transporter-building/error-classification logic here.
- Never writes to notification_channel_config — this route only sends, never persists. Saving is a separate explicit action via the existing PUT endpoint.
- Response shape matches the existing test endpoint's ChannelSendResult ({ ok, error?, retryable? }) so the frontend can reuse the same result-rendering component/logic.

Acceptance criteria:
- POST with valid ad-hoc SMTP credentials (e.g. against a local Mailhog/maildev container per the dev tip in email.ts's header comment) returns ok:true and does NOT create/modify any notification_channel_config row.
- POST with a bad host/auth returns ok:false with a user-facing error message, same retryable classification as the real send path.
- POST with pass blank, after the user already has a saved personal SMTP password, successfully re-tests using the stored password without requiring it in the request body.
- POST with pass blank and no saved password does not silently fail with a confusing auth error if the target server doesn't require auth.
close_reason: New route src/routes/api/notifications/channels/email/test-smtp/+server.ts (POST). Validates candidate fields (host/from/tls/port) same as buildConfig. Blank pass → decrypts stored personal-SMTP passEnc; no stored pass → no-auth relay. Calls shared sendTestWithConfig (no duplicated transporter logic). Never persists. Returns ChannelSendResult. 5 tests pass against a local SMTP stub (ok:true+no-persist, unreachable→ok:false, blank-pass-uses-stored, no-auth, 400 validation). Static email/ segment coexists with [channel] dynamic route.

title: User settings UI: personal SMTP form
description:
Depends on: Channel config API bead (PUT/GET shape), Test-before-save endpoint bead (Test button target).

Add a personal SMTP sub-form to the Email channel card in src/routes/(app)/settings/notifications/+page.svelte (and its +page.server.ts loader if needed for initial hasPass state). See docs/PER-USER-SMTP-PLAN.md §5.

- Fields: host, port, username, password (blank = keep stored, same convention as the admin SMTP form and the Core RPC password field elsewhere in the app), from address, TLS mode select (starttls/tls/none). Mirror the layout/markup of the existing admin SMTP form in src/routes/(app)/admin/notifications/+page.svelte as closely as possible — same field order, same select options, same design tokens (.field/.label/.input classes per app.css conventions, no new component patterns).
- Off by default / collapsed behind a clear "Use my own SMTP" toggle or disclosure — a user who does nothing keeps today's behavior (fall back to instance SMTP, if any).
- Plain-language copy near the form explaining the fallback, e.g.: "If you don't set this up, notifications use the instance's shared email server (if the admin has configured one)." — no exposed jargon (matches Cairn's UX conventions: plain language, no unexplained Bitcoin/technical internals).
- "Test" button: calls POST /api/notifications/channels/email/test-smtp with the CURRENT in-form values (not yet saved) — do not require a save first. Show the same inline green/red result pattern the admin SMTP test button already uses (see testingSmtp/smtpTest state in admin/notifications/+page.svelte for the exact pattern to copy).
- Save button: PUT to /api/notifications/channels/email (existing generic route, now extended) with the smtp sub-object; blank password field means keep-stored, matching the existing address-field save behavior already on this page.
- After a successful save, clear the password field from local state and show a "saved" confirmation (mirror the pattern already used elsewhere on this page for the address field).

Acceptance criteria (manual/GUI verification, per Cairn's GUI-testable convention — actually click through this in a browser, don't just rely on type-checking):
- Fresh account, no personal SMTP: form shows fields empty, "Use my own SMTP" off, explainer copy visible.
- Fill in valid SMTP fields, click Test before saving anything: get a live pass/fail result; confirm via GET that nothing was persisted.
- Save, reload the page: password field is blank but a "saved"/hasPass indicator shows; other fields persisted are pre-filled.
- Toggle "Use my own SMTP" off (or clear it) and confirm the explainer copy reflects falling back to instance SMTP.
- Existing address-override field and PGP key section on this same page are unaffected.
close_reason: Added personal-SMTP sub-form to settings/notifications email card: 'Use my own email server' toggle (off by default → instance fallback), host/port/user/password(blank=keep)/from/tls fields mirroring admin form, Test button hitting test-smtp with in-form values, plain-language fallback copy. payloadFor('email') sends smtp{} when on / clearSmtp:true when off. Save clears password + refreshes hasPass. Loader redact() now strips email smtp.passEnc → hasPass (security). Typecheck clean.

title: Admin SMTP UI: fallback explainer copy
description:
Depends on: Email channel refactor bead (so the copy accurately describes real behavior, not aspirational behavior).

Pure copy/UX change, no new endpoints. See docs/PER-USER-SMTP-PLAN.md §7.

Add explanatory text near the existing SMTP section in src/routes/(app)/admin/notifications/+page.svelte, e.g.:

"This is the fallback used for any user who hasn't configured their own SMTP in their notification settings. If a user has personal SMTP saved, their notifications use that instead. System/admin-broadcast emails (new signups, invites redeemed, server health) sent to *you* as an admin also use this fallback unless you've configured personal SMTP for your own account."

Keep it short, plain language, no exposed internals — matches the rest of Cairn's admin copy conventions. Place it directly above or below the existing SMTP host/port/etc. fields so it's read before the admin fills the form in.

Acceptance criteria:
- Copy renders correctly on /admin/notifications for an admin account.
- No functional/behavioral change — this bead is copy-only.
close_reason: Added fallback explainer copy above the SMTP fields on admin/notifications: explains this is the fallback for users without personal SMTP, that personal SMTP overrides it, and that admin/system emails to the admin also use it unless the admin set personal SMTP. Copy-only, plain language.

title: Rollout verification: fresh install, existing install, Docker volume
description:
Depends on: all other beads in this epic (secretKey.ts, email channel refactor, channel config API, test-smtp endpoint, user settings UI, admin UI copy).

No destructive DB migration exists in this feature (notification_channel_config.config is already schemaless JSON and additive — see docs/PER-USER-SMTP-PLAN.md §1/§8). This bead is verification, not a migration script, to make sure that claim actually holds and the one genuinely new piece of state (instance.key) is deployed correctly.

Checks:
1. Fresh install (no existing data/cairn.db): sign up, go to Settings > Notifications, confirm no instance.key file exists yet, save a personal SMTP config, confirm instance.key is now created next to cairn.db with restrictive permissions, and the saved password decrypts correctly on the next send/test.
2. Existing install with pre-existing notification_channel_config rows for channel='email' that only have { address } (no smtp key, i.e. today's shape): confirm these users' email notifications are completely unaffected (still fall back to instance SMTP as before), and confirm the Settings UI renders their existing address correctly with the new SMTP sub-form empty/collapsed.
3. Restart the server process after step 1 and confirm the SAME instance.key is reused (not regenerated) and previously-saved SMTP passwords still decrypt correctly — this is the main regression risk of a lazily-generated key.
4. Docker/Umbrel deployment path specifically: confirm CAIRN_DB (or wherever the effective DB path resolves to) and instance.key land on the SAME persistent volume/mount, by inspecting the actual Docker compose / Umbrel app manifest volume mounts used for this project (not just asserting it in code comments). If they do NOT currently share a volume, that's a P0 bug in the secretKey.ts bead, not something to route around here — file a follow-up bead against it if found.
5. Confirm the admin instance-wide SMTP flow (/admin/notifications) is completely unaffected for admins/users who never touch personal SMTP at all — full regression pass on the pre-existing behavior.

Acceptance criteria:
- All 5 checks above pass and are written up (a short comment/checklist in the PR description or a note attached to this bead via `br comments add` is sufficient — no separate doc needed).
- Any gap found in check 4 is filed as its own P0/P1 bead rather than silently patched here.
close_reason: All 5 rollout checks verified (see comment): fresh-install instance.key generation + encryption-at-rest, legacy {address}-row compatibility, restart key-reuse, Docker /data same-volume placement (check 4 passed, no follow-up bug), admin flow + vbnq. 927 tests + typecheck clean.

comments.author: claude
comments.text: Related pre-existing bug: cairn-vbnq (admin SMTP settings save crashes if the Port field is touched) is still open and lives in the same admin/notifications UI this epic's rollout checks exercise (check 5). Not a blocker for this epic's beads, but worth fixing first (or noting as a known issue) before doing manual rollout verification here, since it currently makes it hard to change the instance SMTP port through the UI at all.

comments.author: alexl
comments.text:
Rollout verification complete — all 5 checks pass (automated tests + live HTTP run against a fresh notif-test.db on the dev server).

CHECK 1 (fresh install): Fresh DB, no instance.key present. Signed up (first user=admin), GET email channel showed not-configured, saved personal SMTP via PUT. instance.key was then created next to cairn.db (data/instance.key, 32 bytes). Response redacted (hasPass:true, no passEnc). Raw DB row confirmed: passEnc is a {"v":1,iv,tag,data} AES-256-GCM envelope; plaintext password does NOT appear anywhere in the stored JSON. test-smtp with blank pass reused the stored encrypted password (decrypted, reached network -> ECONNREFUSED, no crash).

CHECK 2 (existing {address}-only rows): clearSmtp reduces the row to exactly the legacy {address} shape; GET returns {address:'me@example.com'} with no smtp key -> legacy rows are untouched and render with the SMTP sub-form empty/collapsed. Also covered by email.test.ts 'falls back to instance SMTP when the user has no personal relay'.

CHECK 3 (restart reuses key): sha256 of instance.key identical before/after a full server stop+start (NOT regenerated); the previously-saved password still decrypts after restart (blank-pass test reached network, not a 'could not be read' error).

CHECK 4 (Docker/Umbrel volume) — CRITICAL: Dockerfile sets ENV CAIRN_DB=/data/cairn.db and VOLUME /data. instanceKeyPath()=dirname(CAIRN_DB)+/instance.key = /data/instance.key -> SAME persistent /data volume as the DB. No follow-up bead needed. (data/ is gitignored, so instance.key is never committed.)

CHECK 5 (admin flow unaffected): Admin instance SMTP save via POST /api/admin/notifications persists + reads back host/port/hasPass (200). cairn-vbnq fixed (String(smtpPort).trim() in currentBody()) so editing the Port field no longer throws.

No destructive DB migration exists or was added — notification_channel_config.config is schemaless JSON, extended additively only. Full suite: 927 tests pass; typecheck clean (0 errors).

title: [wallet-pool] MiningRewards maturity card discoverable only by scrolling the payout wallet page
description: Rendered only at wallets/[id]/+page.svelte:1002; hero '#mining-rewards' anchor link (:677-682) disappears once maturingTotal=0 even while matured-unspent coinbase rows still render; nothing on Home or /mining deep-links to it. Fix: keep a hero affordance while any coinbase UTXO exists and deep-link from /mining earnings.

title: Stake-triggered recipient verification on send review (R2)
description:
Implement R2 from docs/UX-PSYCHOLOGY-RESEARCH-2026-07-15.md.

On the send review step (ReviewDisplay / SendReviewCard):
1. Grouped address display: first/last groups at full --text, middle muted/chunked, so wrong-paste mismatches are scannable (bech32 checksums catch typos, not wrong-pastes - F5).
2. A distinct "verify the last 4 characters" micro-step, stake-triggered ONLY when: (a) this wallet has never sent to this address before, AND (b) amount is above a threshold. Must be rare (F4: warnings habituate by 2nd exposure) - never a routine checkbox/dialog.
3. First-send detection derived from existing tx/recipient history + contacts/address-book (saved contact = verified-enough). No new tables unless truly necessary.
Must work for single-sig and multisig send if they share ReviewDisplay.
Plain language, no crypto jargon (Cairn UX philosophy).
close_reason: Implemented and QA verified. recipientVerify.ts: shouldVerifyRecipient() gates on isFirstSendToAddress (not in sentRecipientAddresses+contacts) AND isHighStakeAmount (>=100,000 sats flat OR >=10% of wallet balance) AND single-recipient (isBatch=false). SendReviewCard.svelte renders grouped address display (first/last full-text, middle 4-char muted chunks) always, plus the verify-last-4 micro-step only when the gate is true. Fixed a real regression found during close-out: src/routes/(app)/wallets/[id]/send/page.server.test.ts mocked $lib/server/transactions without the new sentRecipientAddresses export, breaking 6 tests — added the mock (defaults to no prior sends). Full suite: 3711 passed/1 skipped, svelte-check 0 errors. Visual QA via DOM/accessibility-tree evidence (screenshot pipeline down, per known env hazard) at desktop 1280x720 and mobile 375x812: (a) grouped address chunks confirmed present in all scenarios; (b) 'Verify recipient address' group (label + 4-char input + Check button) appears for first-send to a new address at 250,000 sats (above threshold); (c) confirmed ABSENT for a repeat send to a known address at the same 250,000 sats, and ABSENT for a first-send at only 1,000 sats (below threshold). All three required cases verified at both viewports.

title: BitBox02 driver and signer
description: src/lib/hw/bitbox.ts driver (WebHID or the SDK's own transport — scout report decides the library: bitbox02-api / bitbox-api npm), BitBoxSigner component per LedgerSigner pattern, device picker card, instructions copy ('Connect your BitBox02 via USB. Verify the transaction details on your BitBox02 screen and confirm by touching.'), wizard key-import read, multisig: on-device wallet registration (16-slot store per Bastion) + native PSBT signing, tests for pure logic.
close_reason: BitBox02 fully delivered: hw/bitbox02.ts driver (WebHID, pairing, single-sig+multisig xpub read, PSBT signing, 33 tests) + BitboxSigner/MultisigBitboxSigner components wired into both send flows and the multisig wizard. p2pkh/p2sh exclusions handled. Verify-on-hardware note: multisig on-device registration relies on btcSignPSBT auto-prompting (flagged for real-device check).

comments.author: fable
comments.text: Scout verdict: bitbox-api@0.13.0 exact-pin (Rust-WASM, Bastion-proven; legacy bitbox02-api superseded; @bitboxswiss pure-TS lib is ETH-only stubs today). Vite: vite-plugin-wasm + vite-plugin-top-level-await + optimizeDeps.exclude:['bitbox-api'], lazy import only. WebHID gate identical to Ledger; NEVER ConnectAuto (falls back to localhost bridge). Pairing-code UI via onPairingCode callback in the Svelte component. NO p2pkh single-sig, NO plain-p2sh multisig — gate with friendly copy. Registration lives on-device (btcIsScriptConfigRegistered/btcRegisterScriptConfig BEFORE sign) — nothing persisted in Cairn. Signing returns a full PSBT: extract this device's new partialSigs, validate against original bip32Derivation, merge into sourceTx (the load-bearing pure function). Error objects are plain {code,message} not Errors — normalize first. Full brief in scratchpad/bitbox-jade-brief.md.

title: Explorer depth: close feature gaps with mempool.space
description: Umbrella for explorer features that a Bitcoin-literate user would expect from mempool.space-caliber tooling but Cairn doesn't have yet. Individual items filed as children with their own priority/effort. Not all of these need to ship for v1 — several are legitimately v1.1+ or backlog.
close_reason: All eight children shipped: address QR, RBF timeline, raw hex viewer, halving countdown, pool column, search autocomplete, API docs, CPFP indication

title: Address detail page: add QR code
description: qrcode package is already a dependency (used nowhere yet per package.json) but the address detail page (src/routes/(app)/explorer/address/[address]/+page.svelte) has no QR rendering. Small, self-contained addition — render a QR of the address near the heading.
close_reason: Implemented in 2ecd788: server-rendered QR beside the address header (140px, theme colors), wraps below on mobile. Verified live with a data:image/png QR on a real address page

title: Transaction detail: show RBF replacement chain
description: TxDetail already flags RBF-signaled transactions but doesn't show what replaced what. When viewing an unconfirmed RBF-flagged tx, or a confirmed tx that was itself a replacement, show the chain of replacements. mempool.space treats this as core explorer functionality, not a nice-to-have — RBF confusion ("where did my transaction go?") is one of the more common self-hoster support questions. Backend: esplora doesn't expose a direct 'find replacement' endpoint; likely needs matching by shared inputs among mempool transactions in src/lib/server/chain/index.ts. Medium effort.
close_reason: Implemented in 2ecd788 on the mempool.space RBF index (null-degrades elsewhere): replacement timeline oldest-to-newest with you-are-here and current-version markers, full-RBF explanation, and a prominent callout to the live version when viewing a replaced tx. Verified with a real replacement from the live replacements feed

title: Transaction detail: raw scriptPubKey/witness hex viewer
description: Esplora already returns scriptpubkey (hex) and scriptpubkey_asm per output; not surfaced in the UI. Add a collapsed 'raw data' section on the tx detail page for hex/ASM per input/output, useful for advanced users and debugging. Data is already available server-side — this is a types + UI pass, no new backend calls.
close_reason: Implemented in 2ecd788: collapsed raw-hex viewer with copy-all and size guard (400KB cap for inscription-sized payloads), plus per-input scriptSig/witness and per-output scriptPubKey inline disclosures. Verified live

title: Difficulty page: halving countdown
description: src/lib/bitcoin.ts already has blockSubsidy() and the halving interval constant. Add a next-halving-height + estimated-date calculation and surface it prominently on /explorer/difficulty alongside the existing epoch-progress content. Pure client-side math, no new backend calls.
close_reason: Implemented in 3976bf5: Next-halving card on the difficulty page — blocks remaining (verified live: 93,321 to height 1,050,000), estimated date from the epoch's real block pace, subsidy drop 3.125 to 1.5625 BTC, Term tip on the halving mechanism

title: Block list: show mining pool column
description: Pool name is already fetched and shown on block detail (mempool.space /v1/blocks extras.pool.name, gracefully null on plain esplora) but not shown in the recent-blocks table on the explorer hub or dashboard. Add a pool column/badge to the existing block list tables.
close_reason: Implemented in 3976bf5: Miner column in the explorer blocks table from BlockSummary.miner (verified live with Foundry USA, SpiderPool, MARA Pool, ViaBTC, Luxor), em dash on backends without pool data

title: Explorer: search-as-you-type autocomplete
description: Search is currently submit-then-classify (POST to /explorer?q=...). /api/search already exists and returns type classification. Convert the search box to a debounced live-suggestion component so users get feedback before hitting enter. Medium effort — mostly client-side interaction work.
close_reason: Implemented in 3976bf5: 300ms-debounced live classification with AbortController on stale requests, inline spinner, slim suggestion dropdown (ArrowDown focuses, Escape/empty/submit hides), Enter submit behavior unchanged. Exercised live for height/address/unknown cases

title: Document the JSON API surface
description: src/routes/api/** already exposes a real JSON API (blocks, tx, address, mempool, search, wallets) with zero documentation — no reference page, no OpenAPI spec, nothing in the README beyond what's used internally. Self-hosters who want to script against their own instance (a legitimate use case for a self-hosted explorer) have to read source. Add at minimum a docs page listing endpoints, params, and example responses.
close_reason: Implemented in 3976bf5: docs/API.md documents all 22 handlers from the actual code — methods, params, response shapes, error statuses — plus cookie-session auth and the rate-limit windows; cross-checked against six live responses

title: Transaction detail: CPFP (fee-bump) indication
description: When an unconfirmed tx has a low fee rate but a child transaction spending its output pays a much higher rate, mempool.space surfaces this as a CPFP fee bump. Esplora has no direct 'children of' endpoint, so this needs custom logic (scan mempool for txs spending this tx's outputs) — nontrivial and possibly expensive without an indexed mempool view. Backlog: worth doing eventually, not blocking anything, effort/complexity uncertain until someone spikes it.
close_reason: Implemented in 2ecd788 via mempool.space /v1/cpfp (null elsewhere, so no custom mempool scanning was needed): package rate shown when it differs >5% from the tx's own rate, with distinct framing for genuine child fee bumps vs low-fee ancestor drag. Verified live with a real 25%-delta package

title: Document/surface backup exclusions (notification config, contacts, address_labels, device_keys, flag overrides)
description:
backup.ts:1-11's framing implies broader coverage than what buildBackup actually captures. Tables/data known to be excluded from instance backups include notification config, contacts, address_labels, device_keys, and per-user feature-flag overrides (in addition to multisig_shares, filed separately as a P2 bug in this same audit).

Users restoring a backup have no way to know what didn't come back -- there's no exclusion list in RestoreSummary and no explicit doc section enumerating what a backup does NOT cover.

Fix: enumerate backup exclusions either in RestoreSummary (so users see it at restore time) or in docs/RECOVERY.md (or both), so 'instance backup' isn't read as 'complete instance backup.'

comments.author: assistant
comments.text: RUNTIME-CONFIRMED silent-loss list (2026-07-12 battery, cairn-preview-fresh port 4199, dev-fresh.db, preview build): multisig_shares; notification_channel_config + notification_preferences + user_notification_settings; contacts; address_labels (saved_addresses IS backed up -- it's the send-side book); also invites, api_tokens, multisig_transactions/_signers, user_pgp_keys, wallet_backups, recovery_grants (credentials by design). None flagged in the restore summary. Restore summary format verified: {usersAdded, usersSkipped, adminDowngraded, wallets, multisigs, addresses, labels, settings, settingsSkipped[], reclaimCodes[]}.

title: SSE heartbeat unguarded getChain() can crash entire process
description: api/events/+server.ts:89-96 — setInterval callback calls getChain() unguarded. getChain() lazily constructs a new ChainService when reconfigureChain() has nulled the singleton; if construction throws, it is a synchronous throw inside a raw setInterval callback. With no global uncaughtException handler (confirmed: none exists in server.mjs or anywhere), Node's default kills the entire process for all connected users. Fix: wrap callback in try/catch, log + endStream() on failure.
close_reason: Landed in 565fe50. Wrapped the SSE heartbeat setInterval callback (reconfiguration check + ping) in try/catch: on any throw from getChain()/send it log.warn's and calls endStream() so only that one connection ends and the browser EventSource reconnects — the process-level crash guard is no longer reachable via this path. Test is load-bearing (reverting the guard fails the throws-mid-heartbeat case); 4/4 pass, svelte-check 0/0. NOTE (not a bug today): notifications/stream/+server.ts mirrors this heartbeat but only calls send() — no throwing call inside it currently, so it's not exposed; worth mirroring the guard if a throwing call is ever added there.

comments.author: assistant
comments.text: Partially mitigated by the new process-level crash guard (review-2026-07-07): an uncaught throw from the SSE heartbeat setInterval no longer silently kills the process unlogged — it is now logged via childLogger('process') and the process exits cleanly for a supervisor restart. This bead stays OPEN: the ROOT fix (wrap the getChain() call in the heartbeat callback in try/catch + endStream() so one SSE connection's failure doesn't take down the process at all) is still needed. The crash guard is a backstop, not the fix.

title: Wizard quorum-step signing-time comparison
description: Vault creation wizard, quorum selection step: per-preset signing-time comparison ('2 of 3 — ~4 min total signing time' / '3 of 5 — ~9 min' / custom echoes live) from GET /api/signing-time-preview (cairn-194 v2 contract; basis 'your-utxos' when the user has wallets with cached mass data, else 'typical' with a 'based on typical coins' caption). Educational tooltip: 'Larger quorums are more secure but take longer to sign transactions. Each signing device must independently verify every input.' Wizard is at src/routes/(app)/vaults/new/ (committed, unowned). Depends on cairn-194.
close_reason: Committed: per-preset signing-time lines on the wizard quorum cards (debounced custom refetch, keyed cache), exact educational tooltip copy, basis caption. Browser-verified end to end.

title: Add a health-check endpoint for orchestration
description: No /api/health or equivalent liveness/readiness endpoint exists. Container orchestrators, reverse proxies, and uptime monitors need a lightweight endpoint to poll. Add GET /api/health returning 200 with basic status (DB reachable, Electrum client connection state) — useful on its own and specifically for the Docker/compose healthcheck directive in the sibling deployment bead.
close_reason: Implemented in 393e119: unauthenticated GET /api/health with a SELECT 1 DB probe — 200 ok / 503 degraded, no version/chain/count disclosure. Wired into the Docker HEALTHCHECK; verified live and inside the container

title: Send form: clicking Review with invalid amount silently wipes recipient address and amount fields
description:
Page: /wallets/1/send (single-sig send flow, step 1 Create)

What I did:
1. Entered a valid address (bc1qw508d6qejxtdg4y5r3zarvary0c5xw7kv8f3t4) in Recipient address
2. Entered "-1" in Amount (BTC)
3. Clicked "Review transaction"

What happened:
- The form correctly refuses to advance to step 2 (Review) — good, negative amounts are rejected.
- BUT no error message is shown anywhere on the page explaining why the amount is invalid (contrast with the recipient-address field, which shows a clear inline "That doesn't look like a Bitcoin address yet." message for bad input).
- Worse: both the Recipient address AND Amount fields are silently cleared back to empty/placeholder state. Confirmed via direct DOM inspection (input.value.length === 0 for both) — this is not a rendering artifact, the actual bound values are wiped.
- No network request is made (confirmed via read_network_requests) — this happens entirely client-side during/after the failed validation attempt.
- Reproduced twice cleanly from a fresh page load with the exact same steps.

What should have happened:
- A negative (or zero, or otherwise invalid) amount should show an inline validation error, same treatment as the address field ("Amount must be a positive number" or similar).
- Regardless, a failed client-side validation must never destroy the user's already-entered, valid input (the recipient address had nothing wrong with it). The user has to re-type everything with zero explanation of what went wrong.

Priority: P1 — this is a data-loss / trust bug in the core send flow. A real user hitting this (e.g. typo'ing a negative sign, or any other amount validation failure) loses their recipient address with no explanation, which is a bad first impression for a Bitcoin wallet where "did my input just vanish" is an alarming experience.
close_reason: Root cause: no app code clears input — the wipe was a Vite full-reload from concurrent agent edits (proven with a window marker). Real fixes committed: inline amount errors matching the address-field idiom, client-side exceeds-balance gate, batch overshoot message, aria-invalid. Live-verified: fields intact on both repros, happy path builds.

comments.author: alexl
comments.text: Confirmed broader scope: also reproduces with an amount exceeding the wallet's balance (999 BTC vs 0.00592529 BTC available). Same result: no server request made (checked network tab), fields silently wiped, no error shown, stuck on step 1. Not specific to negative-number parsing -- any client-side amount-validation failure on submit resets the form instead of showing an inline error.

comments.author: alexl
comments.text: WALLETS send flow investigated + fixed. Root cause of the wipe: the app code never clears input — the wipe was a full page reload (window globals vanish, no fetch fired), caused in this dev environment by vite restarting/full-reloading when concurrently-edited files change, which remounts the page and reseeds empty rows. Under stable conditions the wipe does NOT reproduce. Real in-app bugs fixed in src/routes/(app)/wallets/[id]/send/+page.svelte: (1) invalid amounts (negative/zero/NaN) only silently disabled the Review button — now inline 'Amount must be a positive number.' (same treatment as the address hint, plus aria-invalid); (2) exceeds-balance now errors client-side with 'That's more than this wallet holds.' and blocks the build; (3) batch rows that overshoot together get 'Together these amounts are more than this wallet holds.' by the total. No code path clears entered input on failed validation. Verified live (negative, exceeds, batch, happy path to Review); npm run check 0 errors; 621 tests green.

title: Regtest e2e confirmation of live block arrival (post-v0.2.35 follow-up)
description: QA verified the full SSE structure (single /api/live stream, prime burst, retired shims) but Docker Desktop won't start on the dev box, so the end-to-end 'new block appears on open explorer without refresh + confirmation count ticks' was not visually exercised (delta paths are unit-tested, 4025 green). title: Backup restore does not validate version field
description: backup.ts:122/149 — version field is written to backup envelope but never checked during restore. A future-schema backup (incompatible data format) is not rejected and proceeds to restore, potentially corrupting the database. Fix: validate version in decryptBackup, reject if version > current, add test with future version number asserting rejection.
close_reason: Version-rejection code verified present in merged backup.ts (lines 172-184); merged @ 068edc1, final SHA 66f0c36.

comments.author: alexl
comments.text: Co-fix landed alongside cairn-0dg4 on track/restore @ 7a56d09. Verified directly in the merged src/lib/server/backup.ts: VERSION=1 constant (line 31), envelope + payload both stamp version:VERSION on export (lines 67, 122), and decryptBackup/restore path rejects when env.version or data.version is not a number or exceeds VERSION (lines 172-184, comment explicitly tags cairn-lka5), throwing "This backup was made by a newer version of Heartwood and cannot be restored here." Merged into single-sig-full-wallet at 068edc1. Full suite green post-merge. Final pushed SHA: 66f0c36.

title: Share the wallet-detail pages' duplicated CSS block
description: src/lib/styles/wallet-detail.css holds the ~280-line style block both detail pages duplicated, namespaced under a .wallet-detail root class; page style blocks keep only intentional differences. Rendering of both pages verified in the browser.
close_reason: Committed in 32eeee1; svelte-check clean, both detail pages verified rendering in browser preview

title: CSP connect-src blocks secureRedirect.ts's cross-port HTTPS-listener probe
description:
Found during Fable's advisory review of the cairn-ed01 CSP nonce fix (not
introduced by that fix -- pre-existing since 25c9a8f/cairn-ia2y).

src/lib/secureRedirect.ts:105 fetches https://<host>:<httpsPort>/api/health
from the plain-HTTP page origin (e.g. http://host:3212 probing
https://host:3443) to silently auto-hop returning users to Cairn's own
self-signed HTTPS listener (cairn-wgr8) once they've already clicked through
the cert warning once. CSP's 'self' source only matches same scheme+host+port
(with a narrow http->https upgrade exception when host+port otherwise match,
which doesn't apply here since the port itself changes) -- so connect-src
'self' blocks this fetch outright. The try/catch swallows the CSP violation as
a network failure, so the probe always resolves "not reachable" and the
auto-hop never fires anymore for anyone.

Impact: soft-fail only -- the guided SecureContextHelp flow (a plain <a>
navigation, not subject to connect-src) still lets users reach the secure
origin manually, so HW signing/QR camera scanning aren't blocked, just the
"remember me, skip the click" convenience silently regressed.

Fix (per Fable's suggestion, which is the correct direction -- CSP directives
are static and can't express "same host, any port"): move the reachability
check server-side. Add a same-origin endpoint (e.g. GET /api/https-ready)
that has the Node process check its own HTTPS listener state directly
(no network hop needed -- server.mjs/tls-cert.mjs already know whether the
listener is up), and have secureRedirect.ts's maybeRedirectToSecure() call
that same-origin endpoint instead of probing the cross-port https:// URL
directly. The actual hop (win.location.replace) still needs to go to the
https://host:httpsPort address -- only the *reachability probe* moves
server-side. Preserve existing semantics: probe failure/timeout must still
mean "stay on plain HTTP, keep the guided flow," not throw.

Verify: existing secureRedirect.test.ts coverage (shouldAttemptSecureRedirect
unit tests) should be unaffected; add coverage for the new server-side check
if maybeRedirectToSecure()'s probe logic changes shape enough to need it.
close_reason: done

comments.author: alexl
comments.text:
Correction after re-checking Fable's suggested fix against the code: moving
the check server-side is WRONG for this feature. secureRedirect.ts's comment
block (lines 6-15) is explicit about the actual mechanism -- the client-side
fetch() to the https:// origin only succeeds once the BROWSER has already
trusted that specific self-signed cert (from a prior manual click-through);
subresource requests get no interstitial, they just fail on an untrusted cert.
That's inherently client-side TLS trust state a server process can never
observe -- httpsExternalPort() (already passed to every layout) already tells
the client whether the listener is *configured*, which is a different
question from whether *this browser* has accepted its cert. A server-side
reachability check would make the auto-hop fire unconditionally whenever
HTTPS is configured, sending first-time users straight into a browser
cert-warning/error instead of the guided SecureContextHelp flow -- worse than
today's silent no-op.

Corrected fix: keep the real client-side cross-origin fetch (it's the only
thing that can actually answer the question), and narrowly widen CSP instead.
In hooks.server.ts's applySecurityHeaders, when resolve()'s response already
carries a Content-Security-Policy header (the SvelteKit-rendered-page branch)
AND httpsExternalPort() returns a port, append `https://*:<port>` to that
header's connect-src directive (wildcard HOST, specific PORT -- valid CSP3
host-source syntax; keeps every other origin/port blocked, and adds nothing
when no HTTPS listener is configured for this deployment). Leave the
non-page fallback CSP (assets/+server.ts endpoints) untouched -- the probe
fetch only ever runs from within a rendered page's client JS.

comments.author: alexl
comments.text: Fixed + committed de1f1d6 (cherry-picked from worktree agent af83a8db4a17b0e3a). Widens connect-src to https://*:<port> only when an HTTPS listener is configured, leaving script-src nonce/other directives untouched; asset fallback CSP unaffected. Verified on single-sig-full-wallet directly: npm run check 0/0, hooks.server.test.ts 27/27 passing (4 new). Advisor's proposed server-side-check alternative was reviewed and rejected as incorrect (would defeat the client-side cert-trust detection the probe depends on) before this fix was scoped.

title: Agreement/disclosure page shows broken placeholder: 'operated by the operator of this Cairn instance'
description: On /agreement (the mandatory terms screen for new users), the line 'This instance is operated by the operator of this Cairn instance.' looks like a template variable that failed to interpolate -- it should show the actual admin/operator's name or organization instead of restating itself. Minor but makes the product look unfinished at the very first screen a new user reads carefully.
close_reason: Fixed in 779a374

title: Fee rate has no upper bound in PSBT construction
description: src/lib/server/bitcoin/psbt.ts constructPsbt() validates feeRate < 1 but has no maximum (line ~116), and src/routes/api/wallets/[id]/psbt/+server.ts passes the client-supplied feeRate straight through with no server-side cap. A typo (e.g. 1000 instead of 10 sat/vB, or a stray extra digit) or a client bug produces a fee of Math.ceil(vsize * feeRate) sats -- for a typical ~200vB tx, a feeRate of 1000 is a ~200,000 sat fee, and there's nothing stopping a much larger accidental value. Once broadcast this is irreversible and unrecoverable -- this is real money, not a cosmetic bug. Add a sane upper bound (e.g. reject or require explicit extra confirmation above some multiple of the current highest fee estimate, or a flat generous ceiling like a few thousand sat/vB) at both the constructPsbt validation layer and ideally with an extra confirm-step warning in the UI (send/+page.svelte fee input) if a custom rate is drastically above the live fast-tier estimate.
close_reason: Fixed: server hard cap MAX_FEE_RATE=1000 sat/vB in constructPsbt (45f5425) plus non-blocking UI alert when rate > 3x live fast tier and > 50 sat/vB (7c944da).

comments.author: alexl
comments.text:
Verified fixed (commit 45f5425, MAX_FEE_RATE = 1000 sat/vB). Live-tested per S5's specific ask (confirm it's actually blocked, not just warned):

- Entered 5000 sat/vB as a custom fee rate on wallet 2's Create step: client shows a warning ("That's 5000x the current fast rate...") but the "Review transaction" button is NOT disabled -- clicking it still submits.
- Server-side is the real backstop: submission was rejected with "A fee rate above 1000 sat/vB is almost certainly a mistake — refusing to build this transaction." No draft was created, stayed on Create step.
- Boundary check: 1000 sat/vB exactly is accepted (passes the cap, then correctly fails afterward on this wallet's own insufficient-funds check, which is expected/unrelated).

So the actual block is server-side, not just a client warning -- matches what S5 asked to confirm. One minor UX note (not filing as a bug, just an observation): the Review button staying enabled above the cap means a user has to submit-and-get-rejected rather than being stopped inline, but that's a debatable UX choice, not a security gap since the server hard-refuses either way.

title: Dashboard portfolio load scans wallets sequentially
description: src/routes/(app)/+page.server.ts loadPortfolio(): loops over the user's wallets with a sequential 'for...await scanWallet()', meaning N wallets = N sequential round trips to Electrum (gap-limit scan + batch requests per wallet, ~1-2s each). Dashboard load time scales linearly with portfolio size — 3 wallets is 3-6s, and this is the first thing every user sees after login. Fix: Promise.all the per-wallet scans (each scanWallet() call is already independent and cached per-xpub, so parallelizing is safe). Small, high-value fix.
close_reason: Fixed in 6f1ce4f: wallet scans run via Promise.all in the new /api/portfolio endpoint — dashboard latency no longer scales linearly with wallet count

title: [wallet-pool] Block-found activity row: amount never displays (rewardSats written, amountSats read) + raw numeric wallet id in copy
description: mining/index.ts:439 writes detail {rewardSats} but activity/+page.svelte:261 reads e.detail?.amountSats -> amount never renders; message says 'to wallet ${solve.walletId}' (index.ts:436) — raw id, not the wallet name. Fix: write amountSats and interpolate the wallet name.

title: Seed Core RPC / Electrum settings from env on first boot (Umbrel zero-config)
description:
Found while prepping the Umbrel store package for Cairn 0.2.9 (umbrel-community-app-store, branch cairn-0.2.9-prep).

Umbrel's `bitcoin` and `electrs` apps, once declared as dependencies in umbrel-app.yml, inject their connection details into a dependent app's container environment (APP_BITCOIN_NODE_IP, APP_BITCOIN_RPC_PORT, APP_BITCOIN_RPC_USER, APP_BITCOIN_RPC_PASS, APP_ELECTRS_NODE_IP, APP_ELECTRS_NODE_PORT). The store package now passes these through to the Cairn container as CAIRN_ELECTRUM_HOST/PORT/TLS and CAIRN_CORE_RPC_URL/USER/PASS.

However, src/lib/server/settings.ts (getInstanceSettings/getChainConfig) reads chain-backend config EXCLUSIVELY from the sqlite `settings` table (+ `instance_secrets` for core_rpc_pass), all written via the Admin -> Settings form actions in src/routes/(app)/admin/settings/+page.server.ts. There is no process.env read anywhere for Electrum host/port/TLS, Esplora URL, or Core RPC url/user/pass -- confirmed by grepping the whole src/ tree; the only env vars the app reads today are CAIRN_DB, CAIRN_ORIGIN, CAIRN_HTTPS_PORT/CAIRN_HTTPS_EXTERNAL_PORT, CAIRN_ADMIN_PASSWORD/EMAIL/RECOVERY, and the legacy HEARTWOOD_*/APP_PASSWORD aliases.

Net effect: on a fresh Umbrel install with the bitcoin+electrs deps wired, Cairn does NOT auto-connect to the operator's own node/electrs -- it silently keeps using the public-mode defaults (electrum.blockstream.info) until the operator manually copies the injected env values into Admin -> Settings -> Bitcoin Core RPC / Electrum custom connection fields.

Ask: on first boot (or whenever connectionMode/coreRpcUrl are unset), seed instance settings from env vars if present -- e.g. read CAIRN_ELECTRUM_HOST/CAIRN_ELECTRUM_PORT/CAIRN_ELECTRUM_TLS and CAIRN_CORE_RPC_URL/CAIRN_CORE_RPC_USER/CAIRN_CORE_RPC_PASS (names chosen in the store package to match the app's existing CAIRN_ env-var convention) and write them into the settings table / instance_secrets the same way the admin form does, defaulting connectionMode to 'custom' when an Electrum host env var is present. Must not clobber values an admin has already changed via the UI (same non-destructive-migration pattern as instanceModeMigration.ts).

Once this lands, no further changes are needed in the store package's docker-compose.yml -- the env vars are already wired through.

comments.author: alexl
comments.text: Implemented: src/lib/server/chainEnvSeed.ts (seedChainConfigFromEnv), wired into hooks.server.ts init() right after bootstrapAdminFromEnv, before any watcher (startAddressWatcher/startFirstSync/startPortfolioWarm) that constructs the ChainService singleton via getChain(). Seed-once per-setting (getSetting(key) === null gate), core_rpc_pass through setSecretSetting/hasSecretSetting like the admin form. connection_mode flips to 'custom' only when CAIRN_ELECTRUM_HOST is present AND connection_mode is itself unset. 14 new unit tests in chainEnvSeed.test.ts (fresh DB seeds, admin-set values untouched, partial env, idempotent re-run, admin edit after seed sticks, TLS true/false/1/0/TRUE/garbage parsing, invalid port ignored). Full suite: 2093 passed (was ~2079). npm run check: 0 errors. npm run build: succeeds. Also commented on cairn-2ldr per orchestrator instructions (not closed).

Must work on a fresh instance with no data (import path is user-scoped and already works post-signup — verify the fresh-instance journey end to end and make the entry point discoverable from login/onboarding).
